title: Ballpoint Ink on Upholstery
timeEstimate: 20–60 minutes home triage; care-code-gated, no overwetting, refusal-biased
stainChemistry: Ballpoint ink on upholstery is concentrated dye plus oily resin binder plus glycol solvent. The resin grips the fabric fiber once dried. Upholstery adds two complications that make this much harder than the same stain on a garment: (1) you can't pull it off and submerge it — every step is in-place, with overwetting risking water rings, foam saturation, and backing damage, and (2) care code rules apply — W (water-safe) tolerates Dawn cleanup, S (solvent-only) does not, WS allows both, X is vacuum / brush only and pro from minute one. The hairspray myth applies just as on garments — modern hairspray polymer leaves a sticky residue that makes the stain worse. The right home tool is 91% isopropyl alcohol on a cloth, with rigid blot-and-extract discipline (no pouring, no scrubbing, never saturate). Refusal bias on upholstery is high — it's easy to make this worse, hard to make it cleaner.
whyThisWorks: 91% isopropyl alcohol on a cloth dissolves the resin and lifts dye onto the cloth. With rigid blot-and-extract discipline (cloth-only application, never pouring, replacing cloth as it picks up ink), the ink transfers OUT instead of spreading. Care-code-gated finish: W / WS allows Dawn damp-cloth clearing, S-only stays dry-buff. The honest cap: ballpoint ink on upholstery often leaves a faint shadow — pro is the right call past a single home pass on most dyed upholstery.
defaultAssumption: Pen leak on couch / armchair / sofa cushion, care code unknown or W. Dyed / patterned / S-only / X-code / vintage / leather upholstery = pro from minute one. Removable washable cushion cover = take it off and treat as garment instead.

spottingProtocol.technique: Care label gate before any chemistry
spottingProtocol.instruction: Find the care code (under cushion, attached tag, manufacturer tag). W or WS = home protocol candidate. S-only = solvent only, no Dawn rinse. X = vacuum only, pro. Leather, vintage, designer pieces, dry-clean-only = pro from minute one. Removable washable cover? Take it off — treat as garment, easier.
Don't reach for hairspray — modern formulas leave sticky polymer residue that makes the stain worse and harder for any pro to clean later. The right home tool is 91% isopropyl alcohol on a cloth.

spottingProtocol.technique: Bleed test on dyed / patterned upholstery
spottingProtocol.instruction: Inside seam / hidden area: dab 91% IPA, blot with white cloth. If color transfers, skip alcohol — pro from there. Patterned upholstery often bleeds — extra caution.

spottingProtocol.agent: 91% IPA Cloth Tamp — Blot and Extract
spottingProtocol.equipment: 91% IPA on white cotton cloth, ventilation
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply 91% IPA to a clean white cloth — NEVER pour on upholstery. Tamp the spot from the top, blot, lift, rotate to a clean section, tamp again. Replace cloth as it picks up ink. Outside-in. STOP if the spot starts SPREADING outward — that means the upholstery dye is bleeding too. 2 passes for stubborn, then assess. Open windows; flammable, no flame.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Dawn + cool water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: W or WS code: Dawn worked into a DAMP (not wet) cloth, press the spot 5 min, blot. Damp-cloth only — never pour. S-only code: skip — dry buff with a clean cloth instead. NEVER saturate the cushion (foam mildew risk inside).

Stack paper towels on the spot, weight overnight to extract residual moisture and ink. Fan-dry the room. Inspect dry — wet upholstery hides residual color that reappears once dry. NEVER hot extractor / steam — heat locks ink.

steps: 1. Care code triage — W/WS only, S/X/unknown/leather/vintage = pro.
3. Hidden-area IPA test on dyed / patterned upholstery.
4. 91% IPA cloth tamp, blot-and-extract, never pour. Stop if spreading.
5. Dawn damp-cloth (W/WS only), or dry buff (S-only). Never saturate.
6. Weighted towel + fan-dry overnight. NO hot extractor / steam.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — hot extractor / steam locks ink
warnings: S, X, unknown care code, leather, vintage = pro from minute one
NEVER hairspray — sticky polymer residue makes worse
NEVER pour solvent / water on upholstery — apply to cloth only
NEVER saturate the cushion — foam mildew inside
NEVER hot extractor / steam until cleared — locks ink
STOP if the spot is SPREADING — pro from there
Hidden-area test on patterned / dyed upholstery before alcohol

homeSolutions.agent: Upholstery Ink Is Refusal-Biased — Easy to Make Worse
homeSolutions.instruction: Ballpoint ink on a sofa or armchair is one of the harder home jobs because every step is in-place — you can't pull the cushion off and rinse it. The biggest risks are spreading the ink, oversaturating the cushion (foam mildew inside), and using the wrong product. If you can take a removable washable cushion cover OFF, do that — it becomes a garment problem, much easier. Otherwise, expect refusal-style: minimal home work, fast escalation if it spreads.

homeSolutions.agent: Find the Care Code Before Anything
homeSolutions.instruction: Look under a cushion or on a hidden tag. W or WS = water-safe, home protocol candidate. S = solvent only, skip the Dawn step. X = vacuum only, pro. Leather, vintage, designer, or dry-clean-only = pro from minute one. No tag = pro.

Older home advice says hairspray works on ballpoint ink. It doesn't anymore. Modern hairsprays are mostly polymer + fragrance; they leave a sticky residue that makes the stain worse AND harder for any pro to clean later. Use plain 91% isopropyl alcohol from the drugstore.

Dab a drop of 91% isopropyl alcohol on an inside seam or hidden area, blot with a white cloth. If color transfers, skip alcohol — pro from there. Patterned and dyed upholstery often bleeds; this test is non-negotiable.

homeSolutions.agent: Alcohol on a Cloth — Tamp, Don't Pour
homeSolutions.instruction: Apply 91% isopropyl alcohol to a clean white cloth — NEVER pour on the upholstery. Tamp the spot from the top, blot, lift, rotate to a clean section, tamp again. Replace cloth as it picks up ink. Outside-in. STOP if the spot starts SPREADING outward — that means the upholstery dye is bleeding too, and continuing makes it worse. 2 passes for stubborn, then stop and assess. Open windows — alcohol is flammable, no flame nearby.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn Damp Cloth — Only on W or WS Code
homeSolutions.instruction: If the code is W or WS: Dawn worked into a DAMP (not wet) cloth, press the spot 5 minutes to clear alcohol residue. Damp-cloth only — never pour. If the code is S: skip — dry buff with a clean cloth instead. NEVER saturate the cushion at any step — wet foam grows mildew inside within days.

homeSolutions.agent: Weighted Paper Towels Overnight Plus Fan-Dry
homeSolutions.instruction: Stack paper towels on the spot, weight with a heavy book, leave overnight. Fan the room. The towels extract residual moisture and any remaining ink. Inspect dry. NEVER use hot extractor or steam at any step — heat locks ink permanently.

Honest cap on upholstery ink: even a single careful home pass often leaves a faint shadow. If two passes haven't fully cleared it, escalate. Tell them: "Ballpoint ink on [W / WS / S / X / unknown / leather] upholstery, age [fresh / hours / days], home-treated with [91% IPA cloth tamp + Dawn damp-cloth (W/WS) + weighted towel overnight]. No hairspray, no saturation, no hot extractor. [Residual shadow / spreading observed]."

steps: 1. Care code triage — W/WS only at home; S/X/unknown/leather/vintage = pro.
3. Hidden-area IPA test on dyed / patterned upholstery.
4. 91% IPA on cloth, blot-and-extract from top, never pour. Stop if spreading.
5. Dawn damp-cloth on W/WS only. NEVER saturate.
6. Weighted paper towels overnight + fan-dry. No hot extractor.
7. Escalate past 2 passes with residual shadow.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop on S, X, unknown care code, leather, vintage, designer pieces — pro from minute one. STOP if spot is SPREADING during alcohol contact — pro from there. Stop on hot-extracted / steamed ink (often permanent). Stop after 2 passes with residual shadow.
warnings: S, X, unknown, leather, vintage = pro
NEVER hairspray — sticky polymer residue worsens stain
NEVER saturate cushion — foam mildew inside
NEVER hot extractor / steam — locks ink
Hidden test on patterned / dyed upholstery

neverDo: Never treat S / X / unknown / leather / vintage upholstery at home
Never use hairspray on ink — myth, sticky polymer residue
Never pour alcohol or water on upholstery
Never saturate the cushion — foam mildew inside
Never hot extractor or steam until cleared
Never continue if the spot is SPREADING
fiberSensitivities: W / WS code synthetic upholstery: tolerates 91% IPA cloth tamp + Dawn damp-cloth
S code: solvent only — skip Dawn, dry buff residue
X code: vacuum / brush only — pro
Vintage / designer / patterned upholstery: pro from minute one
Foam cushions: never saturate — mildew inside within days

materialWarnings: Hairspray is the wrong solvent — modern formulas worsen stain
Never saturate — foam mildew is a worse problem
Stop if spreading — refusal bias is correct
Honest cap: faint shadow often remains; pro past one careful pass

escalation.when: Care code S, X, unknown, leather, vintage, or designer. Spot is SPREADING during alcohol contact. Hot extractor / steam already used (often permanent). Patterned / dyed upholstery where hidden test shows bleed. 2 passes plateau with residual shadow. Cushion shows oversaturation / musty smell from prior cleaning attempts.
escalation.whatToTell: "Ballpoint ink on [W / WS / S / X / unknown / leather / vintage] upholstery, [synthetic / wool / silk / mixed], [removable cover / fixed], age [fresh / hours / days], home-treated with [91% IPA cloth tamp from top + Dawn damp-cloth (W/WS) + weighted towel overnight]. No hairspray, no saturation, no hot. [Residual shadow / spreading observed / oversaturation suspected]."
escalation.specialistType: professional upholstery cleaning service with solvent spotting capability for synthetic upholstery; specialty conservator for vintage / designer / leather / silk / wool

contextQuestions: Care code — W, WS, S, X, unknown?
Removable washable cover or fixed upholstery?
Fresh, hours old, or has hot extractor / steam been used?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Care code gate is everything on upholstery — wrong code + wrong chemistry = ruined upholstery. The hairspray myth is the most common home failure. Foam saturation is the silent failure mode (mildew inside within days). Refusal bias is correct because upholstery ink is hard to fully clear at home and easy to make worse. If a removable washable cover comes off, treat as garment — much easier. Honest cap: faint shadow often remains.
